common_name: Japanese Long Cucumber (Kyuri, Japanese Burpless Cucumber)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is above 65°F; Japanese cucumber thrives on vertical trellising — the climbing vines produce straighter fruits and better air circulation when grown vertically, and trellising is standard practice for this cucumber in Japanese and Asian-style gardens.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost when soil is above 65°F; train vines to a trellis for straight fruits and good air circulation. Japanese long cucumber ('Kyuri' in Japanese) is the standard cucumber of Japanese cuisine and is distinct from both the thicker-skinned American slicing cucumber and the ribbed Chinese varieties — producing slender (1–1.5 inch diameter), uniformly dark green, smooth-skinned fruits 8–12 inches long with exceptionally thin, tender skin that needs no peeling, very small seed cavity, dense crisp flesh, and mild, sweet, refreshing flavor with virtually no bitterness. The thin skin and low cucurbitacin content make it 'burpless' and highly digestible, and the crisp, dense flesh holds its texture remarkably well in sliced preparations. Japanese cucumbers are the standard for pickled cucumber preparations (as in the Japanese salted tsukemono tradition) because of their superior texture, and they are equally outstanding for fresh eating, in salads, and in Korean oi sobagi kimchi. Multiple varieties are grown in Japanese commercial and home horticulture, all sharing the smooth skin, thin wall, dense flesh, and mild flavor characteristics. Production is very heavy when vines are trellised and harvested regularly, and the plants perform well in the hot, humid conditions of the Gulf South summer.
sources: Kitazawa Seed Company Asian Vegetable Guide
Tokyo University Faculty of Agriculture vegetable research publications

common_name: Korean Cucumber (Joseon Oi, Korean Pickling Cucumber)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is above 65°F; Korean cucumber vines benefit from trellising and regular harvesting — pick fruits at 6–8 inches for best flavor and texture for pickle-making, or allow to reach full size for fresh eating.

directions: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost when soil is above 65°F; Korean cucumber (Joseon Oi or 'Korean local cucumber') is the traditional cucumber of Korean agriculture — a light green to whitish-green cucumber with a bumpy, spiny-warted surface (similar to pickling cucumbers but larger and paler), crisp, dense, mildly flavored flesh, very thin skin, and a shape that ranges from short and stout (about 6 inches) to long and slender depending on the local strain. It is the essential ingredient in oi sobagi (cucumber kimchi) where the crisp texture and thin skin allow quick pickling and absorption of spicy, garlicky brine without becoming soft or mushy — texture properties that make Korean cucumber distinctly superior to standard American slicing or pickling cucumbers for Korean-style preparations. The pale color and slightly different flavor profile also distinguish it from Japanese kyuri types; Korean cucumber has a slightly more pronounced flavor with a mild earthiness. Very heat-tolerant and productive under summer conditions; an excellent Asian specialty cucumber for Southern gardens with access to Korean or Asian seed suppliers.
sources: Kitazawa Seed Company Asian Vegetable Guide
Korea Rural Development Administration agricultural publications
University of Hawaii Cooperative Extension — Asian Vegetables

common_name: Taiwanese Flat Cabbage (A Choy, Chinese Flat Cabbage)

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost and transplant as young seedlings; in zone 9, the primary growing season is October–March for spring harvest, as summer heat causes bolting and poor head formation.

directions: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost or direct sow in fall for cool-season production; Taiwanese flat cabbage is a classic East Asian cabbage variety producing distinctly flat, wide, loosely-structured heads (12–16 inches in diameter but only 4–6 inches tall) with extremely tender, delicate leaves — a completely different texture and flavor experience from the dense, hard European round-head cabbages. The tender leaves are less pungent and more delicate than round cabbage, with a slightly sweet, mild flavor that holds up beautifully in quick-cooked preparations like stir-fries, hot pots, and braised dishes, while also working well in fresh preparations like slaws and salads. The flat shape and loose construction mean the heads do not store as long as round types but they are extraordinarily tender and pleasant to eat. Very popular in Taiwanese and Chinese fresh markets, restaurant kitchens, and among Asian-American home gardeners; the quick maturation (60–80 days from transplant) and cool-season preference make it an excellent fall-to-spring crop in the Gulf South.
sources: Kitazawa Seed Company Asian Vegetable Guide
Taiwan Agricultural Research Institute vegetable publications
University of Hawaii Cooperative Extension — Asian Vegetables

common_name: Nozawana (Japanese Turnip Greens, Nozawana Pickled Leaf Turnip)

planting.note: Direct sow in late summer/early fall for fall and winter production; in zone 9 direct sow September–October for harvest November through February. Nozawana is primarily grown as a fall/winter crop for pickling and fresh use, tolerating frost to about 20°F and improving in flavor after light frost.

directions: Direct sow in late summer or early fall in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; nozawana (also called 'nozawa-na' or 'nozawana turnip') is a cool-season Japanese brassica grown primarily for its large, textured, dark-green leaves rather than its turnip root (the root is small and incidental) — the leaves are the classic ingredient in nozawana-zuke, the Nagano Prefecture traditional pickle that is one of Japan's most beloved preserved vegetables. The large, crinkled, somewhat coarse-textured leaves (larger and more substantial than most Asian greens) are harvested whole and salt-pickled in large crocks over winter, developing a complex, slightly funky, fermented flavor that is eaten alongside rice and miso soup throughout Nagano winters. Fresh young leaves can also be blanched and stir-fried or used in miso soup. In zone 9, nozawana grows as a reliable fall-to-winter crop; it tolerates frost well and improves in sweetness after cold weather. A wonderful specialty brassica for gardeners interested in Japanese fermentation traditions and Asian vegetable diversity.
sources: Kitazawa Seed Company Asian Vegetable Guide
Nagano Prefecture Agricultural Technology Institute publications

common_name: Ong Choy (Water Spinach, Morning Glory Greens, Kangkong)

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant rooted stem cuttings after last frost when soil is warm; ong choy is a heat-loving, fast-growing tropical green that produces heavily in warm, moist conditions and is outstanding for the Gulf South summer season when most temperate greens have bolted. Note: Ipomoea aquatica is a federally regulated noxious weed in the US — it is legal to grow for food in contained garden beds or pots but may not be sold live or transported across state lines without a permit in some states.

directions: Direct sow or plant rooted cuttings after last frost in moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; ong choy (also called water spinach, kangkong in Southeast Asia, or 'rau muống' in Vietnamese) is the principal summer green of tropical Asia — a remarkably productive, fast-growing vining plant that produces enormous quantities of tender, mild, spinach-like shoots and arrow-shaped leaves in hot, humid conditions where temperate greens fail. Ong choy is the go-to summer stir-fry green across all of East and Southeast Asia and is extraordinarily common in Chinese, Vietnamese, Thai, Filipino, and Malaysian cooking — most famously in the Thai preparation 'phad phak bung fai daeng' (morning glory cooked with garlic, oyster sauce, and roasted chilies over high flame) and in Vietnamese simple stir-fries with garlic. The flavor is mild, spinach-like, and slightly mucilaginous; stems are hollow (hence the name water spinach, as it grows in or near water in its native habitat), and tender enough to eat whole including stems. Harvest by snipping growing tips; plants regenerate vigorously and yield continuously through the hot season. IMPORTANT: Ipomoea aquatica is a federal noxious weed in the US — confirm local regulations before cultivating outside a contained area.
sources: Kitazawa Seed Company Asian Vegetable Guide
USDA APHIS Federal Noxious Weed List
